metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the subjective clause in AC2 with a concrete observable: e.g., 'the message contains the contributor's @username, the word "thank", the Gerrit URL, the first-change doc link, and the `git format-patch` instruction — verifiable by grepping the workflow's message template against these five required strings.'
findings.finding: AC2 uses the subjective phrase 'reads as a welcome, not a rejection' with no concrete proving command — there is no checklist of required phrases or structural checks that would make this binary-evaluable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2
findings.suggested_fix: AC2 revised: 'The workflow comment template contains: (1) a `@${{ github.event.pull_request.user.login }}` mention, (2) the Gerrit instance URL, (3) the `docs/your-first-change.md` link, (4) the `git format-patch` instruction, and (5) no language implying the contributor's change is unwanted. Verify by reading the template in lockdown.yml and confirming all five elements are present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5` to its exact commit SHA in the `lockdown.yml` spec (e.g. `dessant/repo-lockdown@<sha>  # v5.0.2`), consistent with the repo's existing policy of SHA-pinning all third-party actions (gerrit-verify.yaml).
findings.finding: The plan specifies `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5` by mutable tag only. The repo's existing security practice, established in gerrit-verify.yaml ("All third-party actions are SHA-pinned") and applied to every third-party action reference in that file (e.g. `lfreleng-actions/gerrit-review-action@a1c036a59b94c8ab89de26d378f6a28cc7378b13  # v1.1.2`), requires a commit-SHA pin for all external actions. Specifying a tag without a SHA is an unverified capability claim that the action will behave as observed at authoring time; a tag can be force-pushed.
findings.location: What section — 'Add `.github/workflows/lockdown.yml` using `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5` with `dessant/repo-lockdown@<commit-sha-of-v5.0.2>  # v5.0.2` in the workflow spec. The SHA can be resolved from the v5.0.2 tag at authoring time (e.g. via `gh api /repos/dessant/repo-lockdown/git/ref/tags/v5.0.2`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin `dessant/repo-lockdown` to a full commit SHA (with the semver tag as a trailing comment) in the workflow spec, matching the repo's SHA-pin policy established in `gerrit-verify.yaml`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5` by mutable semver tag, directly contradicting the repo's established security posture: `gerrit-verify.yaml` contains the comment 'All third-party actions are SHA-pinned (tag in trailing comment)' and all third-party action references in that file are SHA-pinned. Using a mutable tag means the action code can change at any time, including a supply-chain compromise.
findings.location: What section — 'Add `.github/workflows/lockdown.yml` using `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5` with `dessant/repo-lockdown@<full-SHA>  # v5.0.2` after resolving the SHA for the v5.0.2 tag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin `dessant/repo-lockdown` to its exact commit SHA (e.g. `dessant/repo-lockdown@<sha> # v5.0.2`) rather than the mutable `@v5` tag, and document the pinned SHA alongside the human-readable version in the workflow file.
findings.finding: The plan references `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5` (a mutable semver tag) rather than a pinned commit SHA. Mutable tags can be silently re-pointed by the upstream author or an attacker who compromises the account, causing the workflow — which runs with a write-privileged `pull_request_target` token — to execute arbitrary substitute code against the repository.
findings.location: ## What — `.github/workflows/lockdown.yml` spec, `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5`
findings.suggested_fix:
Look up the commit SHA for v5.0.2 (e.g. via `git ls-remote https://github.com/dessant/repo-lockdown refs/tags/v5.0.2`) and use:
```yaml
uses: dessant/repo-lockdown@<full-40-char-sha> # v5.0.2
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin `dessant/repo-lockdown` to an immutable commit SHA (e.g. `dessant/repo-lockdown@<sha>  # v5.0.2`) in `lockdown.yml` rather than the mutable `@v5` tag, to prevent supply-chain substitution on this publicly-triggered, write-capable workflow.
findings.finding: The plan pins `dessant/repo-lockdown` at the mutable tag `@v5`, not an immutable commit SHA. On `pull_request_target`, the action executes with base-repo write access whenever any public-internet user opens a PR. A tag can be moved (maliciously or accidentally) to a different commit; GitHub's own security hardening guidance and OSSF Scorecard both require SHA-pinning for third-party actions used in elevated-privilege contexts.
findings.location: What section — `.github/workflows/lockdown.yml` design, line: `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5`
findings.suggested_fix:
Look up the SHA for the v5.0.2 release tag (e.g. via `git ls-remote https://github.com/dessant/repo-lockdown refs/tags/v5.0.2`) and replace the reference:
```yaml
uses: dessant/repo-lockdown@<full-40-char-sha>  # v5.0.2
```
Re-pin whenever upgrading the action version.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `permissions:` block to `lockdown.yml` granting only `pull-requests: write` and `issues: write` (and `contents: read` if needed), revoking all other default write grants.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `pull_request_target` as the workflow trigger but does not include a `permissions:` block. `pull_request_target` runs with a write-privileged GITHUB_TOKEN (it has access to repository secrets and write permissions by default), even for PRs from untrusted forks. Without an explicit minimal-permissions declaration (`pull-requests: write`, `issues: write` only), the workflow violates least-privilege — the auto-granted write token could be abused if `dessant/repo-lockdown` or any future step is compromised.
findings.location: ## What — `.github/workflows/lockdown.yml` spec, `pull_request_target: types: [opened, reopened]`
findings.suggested_fix:
Add at the workflow or job level:
```yaml
permissions:
  pull-requests: write
  issues: write
  contents: read
```
All other permissions default to `none` when any permission is explicitly set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `permissions:` block to the `lockdown.yml` spec naming the minimum required scopes (`pull-requests: write` to close the PR; `issues: write` or `pull-requests: write` to post the comment — confirm against the repo-lockdown action docs) so the workflow is self-documenting and consistent with the repo's existing permission pattern.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a `permissions:` block for the lockdown workflow, but `pull_request_target` workflows need at least `pull-requests: write` (to close the PR) and `issues: write` (to post the comment) — without explicit grants the default GITHUB_TOKEN permissions will block these writes on repos configured with the recommended 'read-only default token' setting.
findings.location: What section — lockdown workflow spec; Acceptance Criteria AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add `permissions:\n  pull-requests: write` (and `issues: write` if the action posts via the issues API) to the workflow spec, consistent with the principle of least privilege demonstrated by all other workflows in this repo.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `permissions: pull-requests: write` (and `contents: read` if needed) block to `lockdown.yml` so the GITHUB_TOKEN on this publicly-triggered workflow is scoped to the minimum required.
findings.finding: The `lockdown.yml` workflow runs on `pull_request_target` — a trigger that executes with base-repo write access in response to any public fork PR — but the plan declares no `permissions:` block. Without an explicit grant, GitHub applies the repository's default token permissions (which may be broad read/write across scopes). Only `pull-requests: write` and `contents: read` are needed; all other scopes should be denied at the workflow level.
findings.location: What section — `.github/workflows/lockdown.yml` design; also Acceptance Criteria AC1
findings.suggested_fix:
```yaml
permissions:
 pull-requests: write   # close PR + post comment
 contents: read          # no other write scope needed
```
Place this at the workflow (top-level) or job level in `lockdown.yml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the E3 audit scope to include all `pull_request`-triggered workflows in `.github/workflows/` — at minimum add `optionality.yml`, `eval.yml`, and `eval-plan-review-container.yml` to the named list, or replace the specific enumeration with a wildcard directive ('audit ALL workflows with a `pull_request` trigger').
findings.finding: The E3 experiment names a specific set of four workflow files to audit for `pull_request`-event dependencies — `(gerrit-verify.yaml, mirror-guard.yml, reconcile-bridge*.yml, test.yml)` — but the actual `.github/workflows/` directory contains three additional workflows with `pull_request: {}` triggers that are absent from this list: `optionality.yml`, `eval.yml`, and `eval-plan-review-container.yml`.
findings.location: What section, E3 experiment: 'Audit `.github/workflows/*` (gerrit-verify.yaml, mirror-guard.yml, reconcile-bridge*.yml, test.yml) for `pull_request`-event dependencies'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical enumeration in E3 with: 'run `grep -rl "pull_request" .github/workflows/` to enumerate ALL affected workflows' — which yields gerrit-verify.yaml, mirror-guard.yml, reconcile-bridge*.yml, test.yml, optionality.yml, eval.yml, and eval-plan-review-container.yml.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC5 exactly where the non-goal is recorded, e.g., 'in the `## Non-goals` section of CONTRIBUTING.md' or 'as a comment in lockdown.yml header', so the criterion is evaluable by inspecting a named repo artifact.
findings.finding: AC5 ('GerritBot-style auto-import recorded as an explicit non-goal with rationale') does not specify which artifact (file + section) records this, making it unverifiable without guessing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5
findings.suggested_fix: AC5 revised: 'A `## Non-goals` entry in CONTRIBUTING.md records GerritBot-style auto-import as an explicit non-goal with the rationale stated in the Scope section (Go's 34% vs 78% merge-rate data, dormant plugin). Verifiable: `grep -A5 "GerritBot" CONTRIBUTING.md` returns the rationale text.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5` input key names (`close-pr`, `lock-pr`, and the comment input) against the v5 action's `action.yml` before writing the workflow — observe them directly, do not infer from prior-version docs.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5` inputs `close-pr: true` and `lock-pr: false` but does not verify these are the actual input key names in the v5 action manifest. If v5 renamed or restructured its inputs (e.g., the action uses `close` instead of `close-pr`), the workflow will silently do nothing — PRs will not be closed and contributors will receive no message.
findings.location: What section — lockdown workflow spec: '`close-pr: true`, `lock-pr: false` (LibreOffice pattern')
findings.suggested_fix: Read the raw action manifest at `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dessant/repo-lockdown/v5/action.yml` (or the SHA-pinned equivalent) and confirm each input key name, then add the SHA pin to match the repo's existing security posture (`gerrit-verify.yaml`: 'All third-party actions are SHA-pinned (tag in trailing comment)').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering verification of the GitHub repo description update (e.g., 'The repo description reads "Read-only mirror — code review at <Gerrit URL>"').
findings.finding: The 'Update the GitHub repo description to carry the mirror banner' deliverable described in the What section has no corresponding acceptance criterion — it is uncovered work.
findings.location: What section (last bullet): 'Update the GitHub repo description...'; Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] The GitHub repository description reads "Read-only mirror — code review at https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com" (or equivalent mirror signal).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-flight step (or an E3 sub-check) to verify `docs/your-first-change.md` exists in the repo; if absent, add 'create/stub that doc' to the IN scope or replace the link with the CONTRIBUTING.md anchor in the bot message.
findings.finding: The bot comment deep-links `docs/your-first-change.md` but the plan never verifies or specifies creating this file; if it does not already exist, every redirected contributor hits a 404 — a stranded reference.
findings.location: What section — bot comment spec: 'deep-link Gerrit (GitHub OAuth sign-in) + `docs/your-first-change.md`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to E3 (or as E5): 'Confirm `docs/your-first-change.md` exists; if not, create a minimal stub (purpose, Gerrit sign-in link, `git format-patch` quick-start) and add it to IN scope.' Alternatively, change the bot message to link directly to the CONTRIBUTING.md shepherded-patch section until the first-change doc is confirmed to exist.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide and document whether collaborator/maintainer PRs should be exempted from auto-close (dessant/repo-lockdown's `exclude-collaborators: true` option), and record the decision in the workflow YAML as a comment.
findings.finding: The plan does not address whether maintainer or collaborator-opened PRs (e.g., test PRs opened by the repo owner to validate the workflow) should be exempted from the auto-close bot. The `pull_request_target: [opened, reopened]` trigger fires for ALL openers, including the maintainer.
findings.location: 'What' section — lockdown.yml trigger specification
findings.suggested_fix: Add `exclude-collaborators: true` to the repo-lockdown action inputs (or explicitly document why exclusion is undesired, e.g., 'all PRs close so the maintainer tests via a separate scratch repo').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that reopens the test PR (or a second test PR) after the bot closes it and confirms the reopened PR is also closed and commented within ~1 minute, exercising the `reopened` trigger.
findings.finding: The `pull_request_target: types: [reopened]` trigger is specifically cited as closing the Wikimedia reopen loophole (T374157), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies the reopen scenario — only the `opened` case is tested. If the `reopened` type is misconfigured or silently dropped, the loophole remains open and no AC would catch it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first AC bullet; 'What' section — reopened type paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC section: '[ ] After the bot closes the test PR, reopen it from the fork; confirm the workflow fires again: the PR is re-closed (not locked) and the welcome comment is re-posted within ~1 minute, validating the `reopened` type and closing the T374157 loophole.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `docs/your-first-change.md` to the IN-scope deliverables list (with creation spec), or replace the deep-link in the bot comment with an existing file (e.g., `CONTRIBUTING.md` or `docs/local-dev-env.md`) that currently exists.
findings.finding: `docs/your-first-change.md` does not exist in the repository, but the plan's bot-comment message and AC both deep-link it as a live resource contributors will follow immediately after being redirected.
findings.location: What section (bot comment spec): 'deep-link Gerrit (GitHub OAuth sign-in) + `docs/your-first-change.md`'; AC line: 'The message names Gerrit + the first-change doc + the patch fallback'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `docs/your-first-change.md` creation to the Scope/IN list, or point the bot comment at the existing `CONTRIBUTING.md` (which already covers Gerrit sign-in, clone, hook install, and the contribution loop).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either confirm `docs/your-first-change.md` exists (or will be created as part of this plan's scope) before the bot comment goes live, or replace the deep-link with an existing destination (e.g., `CONTRIBUTING.md`) so the redirect message never points to a 404.
findings.finding: The bot comment message deep-links `docs/your-first-change.md`, but this file does not exist in the repository (confirmed: not present in docs/ listing or any search result). A broken link in the welcome message directly undermines the plan's stated "warm welcome, not rejection" goal — every redirected contributor who clicks it hits a 404.
findings.location: What section — bot comment message; AC line: 'The message names Gerrit + the first-change doc + the patch fallback'
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'create `docs/your-first-change.md`' to the In-scope list (or substitute the link target with the already-present `CONTRIBUTING.md`) and add a corresponding AC line verifying the link resolves.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a stated default remediation to E3: e.g., 'if a dependency is found, adapt the conflicting workflow's trigger (e.g., replace `pull_request` with `pull_request_target`) and document the change; if incompatible, file a follow-up ticket and note the constraint before landing the lockdown workflow.'
findings.finding: E3 is a 'check if' placeholder — it says 'audit workflows for pull_request-event dependencies so the posture change breaks no existing automation' but does not state what the executor should DO when a dependency is found; the remediation decision is deferred to the executor.
findings.location: Pre-flight experiments, E3 bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the E3 bullet: 'If a dependency is found: (a) adapt the trigger to `pull_request_target` or add a guard condition; (b) if adaptation is non-trivial, file a blocking sub-ticket and do NOT land the lockdown workflow until resolved. If no dependency is found: record "no conflicts found" as the ticket comment and proceed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in CONTRIBUTING.md or a workflow comment) that the Gerrit URL is intentionally repeated in N places and list them, so any future change is a known multi-file update — or centralise it as a workflow-level `env:` variable referenced by the comment template.
findings.finding: The Gerrit URL (`https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com`) and bot message text are implicitly hardcoded in the workflow YAML with no single source of truth; when the URL or message evolves, maintainers must hunt and update multiple files (YAML workflow, CONTRIBUTING.md, repo description) independently.
findings.location: What section — bot comment spec and repo description; Scope IN list
findings.suggested_fix: In `lockdown.yml`, define `env: GERRIT_URL: https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com` at the workflow level and reference it in the comment body via `${{ env.GERRIT_URL }}`. Add a comment noting CONTRIBUTING.md and repo description also carry this URL and must be updated together.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a failure-observability and fallback path for the lockdown workflow: document a manual-triage cadence (e.g., weekly open-PR audit) and/or add a workflow `on: workflow_run` or failure-notification step so the maintainer is alerted when `lockdown.yml` errors, preventing silent accumulation of unanswered PRs.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `dessant/repo-lockdown@v5` as an external GitHub Actions dependency but specifies no fallback or observability path for workflow failures (e.g., GitHub API rate-limit, action runtime error, permissions regression). When the workflow silently fails, a PR sits open with no welcome comment — reproducing the 'silent ignore' anti-pattern the plan explicitly rejects — and no operator signal beyond default GitHub failure emails, which are easily missed.
findings.location: What section — lockdown.yml workflow definition; Acceptance Criteria line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `jobs.<job>.steps` entry that posts a fallback comment or creates a GitHub issue when the repo-lockdown step fails (`if: failure()`), and document in the plan that a periodic open-PR audit (e.g., monthly) serves as the manual backstop if the workflow is broken.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify failure handling for the lockdown workflow: add a required `permissions: pull-requests: write` declaration in the workflow YAML, and add a step or AC entry for alerting/logging when the action fails to close or comment (e.g., workflow job failure surfaces in GitHub Actions UI and is monitored).
findings.finding: The plan only specifies the happy-path outcome for the lockdown workflow (PR gets a comment and is closed within ~1 minute) but does not address what happens when the GitHub Action itself fails — e.g., GITHUB_TOKEN lacking `pull-requests: write` permission, dessant/repo-lockdown runtime error, or an Actions outage. In those cases, the PR sits open and unanswered, which is precisely the 'silent-ignore' posture the plan is trying to avoid.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet; 'What' section — lockdown.yml specification
findings.suggested_fix: In lockdown.yml, add `permissions: pull-requests: write` at the job level. In the AC, add: 'If the workflow run fails, the failure is visible in the Actions tab and does not silently leave the PR open (verified by injecting a permissions error in the scratch repo during E2/E3 experiments).' Also wire up a `jobs.<job>.if: always()` step that comments a fallback message or posts to an issue if the close step fails.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
